compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
## Plan-review + security-review status (handoff)

**Design provenance:** brainstorm design-of-record is session log `emanatory-confounded-dodo`; the full journey (investigation, decisions, 3-reviewer security panel, and the round-by-round plan-review remediation) is captured in a follow-up session log recorded at handoff.

**Plan-review status (source of truth = `rebar verify-signature <id> --kind plan-review`; NOTE: `--output json` stdout was frequently lost to empty files during this session — the signed attestation on the ticket, not the console JSON, is authoritative):**
- Epic + S1 (holy-heartfelt-pheasant) + S2 (emersed-prettying-eagle) + S3 (lost-nonproift-stilt) + S4 (turbid-isogonal-iguana) + S5 (trogonoid-browny-blowfish) + S6 (witching-subjective-skylark): **PASS + signed** at last review.
- S7 (fixed-jockeyish-swan): **plan review NOT yet run** — its description is authored (incl. the accepted port-configurability note); run `rebar review-plan fixed-jockeyish-swan` before claiming it.

**Higher-effort security-review pass folded in.** A 3-reviewer adversarial panel (coherence + peer-alignment + red-team security) ran over the whole graph and produced a consolidated redesign now reflected in the descriptions. Load-bearing security decisions the implementer MUST preserve:
- The **composite `TokenVerifier` is the single audience/fail-closed choke point** — the SDK's `BearerAuthBackend` never inspects `.resource` (grounded vs mcp 1.28.1), so the composite re-validates every returned token's `resource == auth_resource_server_url` (RFC 8707). No verifier (incl. custom) can bypass it.
- **One resource, one audience:** a single `auth_resource_server_url`; per-verifier `audience` params were DROPPED to close a real hole.
- Fail-closed everywhere (raise = non-acceptance, never acceptance); unknown strategy / empty composite / no-verifier-over-HTTP / unauth-HTTP-without-ack all refuse to start.
- Transport hardening: DNS-rebinding/Origin on by default w/ explicit loopback defaults; non-loopback requires allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack; discovery URLs derive from configured `auth_resource_server_url` (behind-proxy fix), not the request scheme.

**Cross-ticket / infra notes:**
- **Orthogonal to epic `2141-0ba0-5313-48d4` (audit-trail UI).** That is a FastAPI browser dashboard (`src/rebar/audit/server.py`, `[reviewbot]` extra) — a DIFFERENT stack (FastAPI vs mcp SDK), protocol (HTML vs MCP JSON-RPC), audience (human vs agent), files, and config. No dependency edge or shared abstraction is warranted (would be premature coupling). Only incidental overlap: both land on Starlette+uvicorn, and both default to loopback `:8000` — S1's `http_port` is configurable to avoid the clash (noted in S7 docs). If de-dup is ever wanted, the target is the 3 FastAPI apps (review_bot, opcert_service, audit UI), NOT the mcp SDK transport.
- **SDK API confirmed vs mcp v1.28.1:** `TokenVerifier`/`AccessToken(...,resource,...)`/`AuthSettings(issuer_url singular, no authorization_servers field)`/`TransportSecuritySettings`/`streamable_http_app()` all real; SDK's `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` is EXAMPLE-only (S4 writes its own). Pin `mcp>=1.28.1,<2` (S1 owns it, in BOTH the `mcp` and `[dev]` extras).
- **Doc generators need fixing (S1 owns):** `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` hardcodes the gate-var tuple → must iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`; `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` AST-scans literal `os.environ` → must ingest `MCP_ENV_VARS`. S7 regenerates docs for the full key set.
- **Config is FLAT:** `_config_schema.py`'s `coerce_sparse` handles only one-level scalar keys (no nested `[mcp.auth]`, no list-of-tables). All auth config is flat `mcp.auth_*` scalars; the static-token records live in a verifier-parsed secrets file. TOML key `auth_x` ≠ env var name `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_X` (register the ENV-VAR names in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`).

compiled_state.description:
# Optional authentication for the rebar MCP server (OAuth 2.1 Resource-Server model)

## Context

`rebar-mcp` (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py`, `mcp.server.fastmcp.FastMCP("rebar")`) is today
**stdio-only**: `build_server().run()` is called with no transport argument. It has **no HTTP
transport and no per-request/session plumbing**. Per the MCP authorization spec, authentication is
an **HTTP-transport concern** — stdio servers *should not* authenticate (credentials come from the
environment). rebar has deliberately kept MCP stdio-only and met every remote need with separate
FastAPI library-mode services (the Gerrit `review_bot`, the in-flight op-cert service), so
**nothing consumes rebar-mcp over the network today** (Gerrit and GitHub Actions use the rebar
library/CLI in-process, per ADR-0007).

This epic adds an **optional** capability: rebar-mcp can serve over HTTP and require
authentication, acting as an **OAuth 2.1 Resource Server** that validates inbound bearer tokens and
advertises RFC 9728 Protected Resource Metadata. The capability is generic (provider-agnostic) so
any organization can front it with its own identity provider — it is **not** coupled to this
project's dev auth model. Design-of-record: session log `emanatory-confounded-dodo` plus the
higher-effort coherence/peer/security review pass captured in the same log.

## Why (beneficiary + problem)

**Beneficiary:** an operator/organization (including rebar itself, as future follow-on) that wants
to expose rebar MCP tools to remote MCP clients (Claude Code / an IDE / a CI job) over the network
instead of a local stdio subprocess. **Problem today:** there is no HTTP transport and no
authentication, so a shared/hosted rebar store cannot be reached by a remote MCP client without
exposing it unauthenticated. This epic makes a remote rebar-mcp endpoint possible and safe to
require auth on, verified locally.

## Approach & rationale (positive)

Build rebar's **own** token verifiers against the **official `mcp` SDK's `TokenVerifier` seam**
(`mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier.verify_token(token) -> AccessToken | None`), wired via
`FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))`, and serve the optional Streamable-HTTP transport
the SDK already provides. rebar is a **Resource Server only** — it validates tokens and advertises
discovery metadata; it is **not** an Authorization Server and does **not** implement Dynamic Client
Registration (identity is delegated to the deploying org's IdP).

**Why this fits:** (a) the official SDK — already a dependency — provides the `TokenVerifier` seam,
`AuthSettings`, RFC 9728 metadata routes, `TransportSecuritySettings`, and Streamable-HTTP transport
natively; the verifiers are small modules on a stable, blessed seam. (b) It is the mainstream
self-hosted path (GitHub, Grafana, Neon, GitLab, and the SDK's own `simple-auth` example build auth
on the official SDK; GitHub's remote server likewise delegates identity and does not do DCR). (c) It
avoids the standalone `fastmcp` framework, whose `server.auth` module is semver-exempt and has had
multiple CVEs. (d) It keeps every line under rebar's own tests/CI/version control.

**RS-only + no-DCR is defensible, not a usability gap.** Claude Code / Claude.ai do not *require*
DCR: they auto-discover via the RFC 9728 PRM rebar serves (pointing at the org IdP), and when the
IdP lacks anonymous DCR (Entra, Cognito, default Keycloak/Auth0/Okta) the user falls back to
first-class **manual `--client-id`/`--client-secret`** or a **static bearer `--header`** — both of
which rebar's PRM + static verifier serve directly. S7 documents these three connection paths.

## Security (this epic crosses a trust boundary — the core of the plan)

Enabling HTTP exposes rebar-mcp to **lower-trust network actors**. The posture is first-class and
propagated to every child:

- **Authentication is the deliverable.** With auth enabled, every request is admitted only on a
  validated bearer token; an unauthenticated request receives **401 + `WWW-Authenticate`** with the
  RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL. Identity is delegated to the org IdP; rebar mints no tokens.
- **The composite verifier is the single audience/fail-closed choke point.** The SDK does **not**
  validate token audience and `AccessToken.resource` defaults to `None`, so there is no SDK
  backstop. Therefore the **composite** re-validates every returned token: it rejects unless
  `AccessToken.resource` is present **and** equals the single configured `auth_resource_server_url`
  (RFC 8707). Every verifier — including static and trusted-proxy, which have no natural token
  audience — must populate `.resource` explicitly. A verifier cannot bypass the audience check.
- **One resource, one audience.** There is a single `auth_resource_server_url`; it is what the PRM
  advertises AND the audience every verifier validates against — they are never independent.
- **Fail-closed everywhere:** any verifier that raises is logged and treated as **non-acceptance**
  (never acceptance); if no verifier returns a valid, audience-matching token the request is denied
  (401). Misconfigured auth, an unknown `auth_strategies` entry, `auth_enabled=true` with an empty
  composite, or auth-enabled-over-HTTP with no verifier wired all **refuse to start**. An HTTP
  transport with auth disabled refuses to start unless `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP` is set.
- **Transport hardening:** DNS-rebinding/`Origin` protection on by default with explicit loopback
  defaults; loopback bind by default; a non-loopback bind requires explicit host/origin allowlists
  AND an explicit TLS-at-edge acknowledgment (bearer tokens/secrets must never cross the network in
  cleartext). Discovery URLs derive from the configured `auth_resource_server_url` (validated https
  for non-loopback), not the request scheme — the behind-a-proxy correctness fix.
- **No token passthrough** to any upstream (confused-deputy prohibition); tokens/secrets never
  logged (a redaction layer is required and tested).
- **Authenticated ≠ authorized (named residual).** Any authenticated principal wields rebar's full
  ambient authority (writes auto-push; `reconcile live` uses rebar's own git/Jira creds) gated only
  by server-level `required_scopes` and the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/
  `ALLOW_LLM` env gates — which now also gate remote actors. Per-tool scope→capability authorization
  is deferred and documented as a residual risk. Rate-limiting/brute-force protection is delegated
  to the edge proxy in v1, with mandated high-entropy static tokens; documented as a residual.

## Scope

**In scope:** an optional Streamable-HTTP transport; a provider-agnostic auth framework (composite
`TokenVerifier` that is the single audience/fail-closed choke point + config-driven selection)
shipping five verifiers (static bearer, JWKS/JWT, RFC 7662 introspection, trusted-proxy passthrough,
pluggable custom); RFC 9728 metadata + 401; the security/transport hardening above; local
verification (pytest); docs + an ADR; config/manifest updates (`McpConfig`, `MCP_ENV_VARS`,
`server.json`, regenerated `mcp-reference.md`/`env-vars.md`).

**Out of scope (explicit):** deploying an authenticated endpoint on AWS / `rebar.solutions.navateam.com`
and any Gerrit/CI reconfiguration (Gerrit and CI do not use MCP — deliberate follow-on); rebar
acting as an Authorization Server / DCR / token issuance; per-tool scope→capability authorization
(server-level `required_scopes` only); rate-limiting inside rebar (edge-proxy responsibility in v1);
GitHub-**user** and Gerrit authentication (neither is an OAuth token issuer — only a deploying org's
proxy/custom concern). stdio remains the default transport and is unchanged.

## Success Criteria

- A rebar-mcp operator can run the server over HTTP with authentication **required**, using any of
  the five shipped verifier modes, configured entirely through the existing flat `[mcp]` config/env
  surface — with stdio still the default and auth off by default.
- The trust-boundary guarantees hold and are demonstrated locally: unauthenticated → 401 +
  `WWW-Authenticate` + served PRM; valid token → 200; insufficient scope → 403; a token whose
  `resource` is absent or ≠ `auth_resource_server_url` is rejected at the composite regardless of
  which verifier produced it; a disallowed `Host`/`Origin` → 421/403.
- Misconfiguration and downgrade are fail-closed: unknown strategy, empty composite with auth on,
  auth-enabled-HTTP with no verifier, or unauthenticated HTTP without the explicit ack all refuse to
  start; non-loopback bind requires allowlists + the TLS-at-edge ack.
- A third-party operator can connect an interactive Claude client (documented paths) and the design
  is provider-agnostic (no rebar-specific IdP assumptions).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All child stories (S1–S7) are closed.
- [ ] `rebar-mcp` serves over an optional Streamable-HTTP transport while stdio remains the default
      and default behavior is byte-unchanged when the new flat `mcp.*` keys are unset (observable:
      the existing `tests/interfaces/facades/test_mcp.py` stdio suite still passes).
- [ ] With auth enabled, the local suite demonstrates 401-without-token (with a `WWW-Authenticate`
      header carrying the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL), a served PRM document, 200-with-valid-
      token, 403-insufficient-scope, and rejection of a token whose `resource` ≠ `auth_resource_server_url`
      enforced at the composite.
- [ ] All five verifier modes (static, JWKS/JWT, introspection, trusted-proxy, custom) are
      selectable via the flat `mcp.auth_strategies` list / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars, each has passing
      tests, and each populates `AccessToken.resource` so the composite audience check applies.
- [ ] Fail-closed startup is tested: unknown `auth_strategies` entry, `auth_enabled` with an empty
      composite, auth-enabled HTTP with no verifier wired, and HTTP with auth disabled and no
      `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP` all refuse to start.
- [ ] New flat config keys and env vars are reflected in `MCP_ENV_VARS` and `server.json` (the CI
      manifest drift gate `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes) and in regenerated
      `docs/mcp-reference.md` / `docs/env-vars.md`.
- [ ] `docs/mcp-auth.md` and an ADR document the resource-server-only model, the composite audience
      choke point, the five verifier modes, the security posture (incl. the authenticated≠authorized
      residual and rate-limiting delegation), the behind-a-proxy deployment (TLS + allowlists +
      discovery URLs), and the three interactive-client connection paths.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the final integrated branch.

## Testing strategy (children carry the tiers/oracles)

Verification is entirely local (no AWS). Each verifier is unit-tested against its own real logic
(valid/invalid/audience/expiry/algorithm-confusion/rotation/fail-closed/header-spoofing cases), and
the HTTP+auth surface is integration-tested by driving the SDK's Streamable-HTTP ASGI entrypoint
with an in-process `httpx` `ASGITransport` client (asserting HTTP status, the `WWW-Authenticate`
header, and the served PRM document). Each child story names its test tier and observable oracle.

## Compatibility & rollback

Purely **additive**: stdio stays the default, auth is off by default, all new behavior is gated
behind new, unset-by-default flat `mcp.*` keys, so existing stdio deployments and the existing MCP
tests are unaffected. The dependency changes are pinning `mcp>=1.28.1,<2` (the v2-alpha renames the
SDK surface; 1.28.1 is at/above the DNS-rebinding fix) and adding `pyjwt[crypto]` for the JWKS
verifier. Rollback is config-level: leave the new keys unset (or `mcp.transport=stdio`) for exactly
today's behavior.

file_impact.reason: transport selection + auth wiring in build_server/main
new: composite TokenVerifier + five verifier implementations

compiled_state.title: Optional authentication for the rebar MCP server (OAuth 2.1 Resource-Server model)
